attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman is working as a surgical trainee at a metropolitan hospital. Her supervising surgeon notices operating details in the trainee's logbook that do not match her recollection, with what appears to be forged signatures. Another surgeon in the unit reports similar concerns. When questioned by her supervisor, the trainee admits to forging the signatures, stating she did not want to inconvenience the surgeons.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Remove the falsified entries from the logbook
Refer to the hospital executive committee
attributes.explanation:
This case involves deliberate falsification of medical documentation, which represents a significant departure from accepted professional standards. Healthcare practitioners have a legal obligation to report certain types of conduct to the Medical Board of Australia.

Contact the Medical Board is the most appropriate first step as this constitutes 'notifiable conduct' under the National Law. The deliberate forgery of signatures and fabrication of clinical experience represents dishonest conduct that could place the public at risk of harm by misrepresenting a practitioner's actual clinical experience and competence.

Record an unsatisfactory performance assessment may be appropriate as part of managing the situation locally, but does not fulfill the mandatory reporting obligation. Performance issues alone do not necessarily trigger mandatory reporting unless they represent a significant departure from accepted standards.

Suspend the trainee pending investigation may be a reasonable workplace response, but should occur after notification to the Medical Board and in consultation with relevant authorities. Suspension alone does not address the regulatory obligations.

Refer to the hospital executive committee is insufficient as the primary response. While hospital management should be informed, the mandatory reporting obligation to the Medical Board takes precedence and cannot be delegated.

Remove the falsified entries from the logbook would be inappropriate as it could constitute covering up evidence of notifiable conduct. This could itself represent unprofessional conduct by the supervisors.

The Medical Board needs to be notified to ensure appropriate investigation and protection of public safety. Additional local responses such as suspension or performance management may then follow as required.
learningPoints: Forgery of medical documentation constitutes notifiable conduct requiring mandatory reporting to the Medical Board.
Healthcare practitioners have a legal obligation to report significant departures from accepted professional standards that place the public at risk.
Attempting to cover up or failing to report notifiable conduct may itself constitute unprofessional behavior.
references: www.medicalboard.gov.au. (n.d.). Medical Board of Australia - Guidelines for mandatory notifications. [online] Available at: https://www.medicalboard.gov.au/Codes-Guidelines-Policies/Guidelines-for-mandatory-notifications.aspx.
